verdict_reasoning: ATP5F1B encodes the beta subunit of the mitochondrial F1Fo-ATP synthase. Its canonical localization is the mitochondrial inner membrane matrix side — it has no TM domain of its own and no GPI anchor. However, a well-documented body of literature establishes ectopic surface display: (1) OMIM records that the beta subunit (ATP5B/ATP5F1B) was identified as a high-affinity HDL/apoA-I receptor on the hepatocyte plasma membrane, with experimental confirmation of ATP hydrolase activity at the cell surface. (2) ATP5F1B is found on the PM of endothelial cells, adipocytes, keratinocytes, hepatocytes, and multiple cancer cell types. (3) A 2023 Communications Biology study shows ATP synthase is assembled in mitochondria, then transported to the PM via microtubule/DRP1/KIF5B-mediated mitochondrial membrane fusion. (4) ATP5F1B was directly shown on leukemia cell surfaces interacting with platelet glycoprotein IIb, and on highly metastatic prostate cancer (PC-3M) cells confirmed by flow cytometry and immunoelectron microscopy. The surface pool is most prominent in cancer and other stress/metabolic contexts, qualifying this as cell_state_induced contextual display. The protein lacks its own PM anchor but reaches the outer leaflet via the mitochondrial membrane fusion mechanism and is retained as a complex.
predicted_key_uncertainty: The precise breadth of normal (non-cancer) cell types with constitutive ectopic surface display vs. cancer/stress-restricted display is not fully resolved, but surface accessibility itself is firmly established.
